content.full_text:
.

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

March 22, 1989

Mr. Garland R. Porter
Treasurer, Committee to Re-Elect
Mayor Bill Carroll
267 Raleigh Drive
Vacaville, CA 95687

RE: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-89-078

Dear Mr. Porter:

You have requested advice under the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/

FACTS

You are the treasurer for the Committee to Re-Elect Mayor
Bill Carroll. Mr. Carroll is currently the mayor of the City of
Vacaville. Mr. Carroll will be seeking the office of supervisor
for the 4th District, Solano County in a June, 1990, election.
Mr. Carroll wants to retain his current mayoral committee's
identification number and amend the committee's Statement of
Organization, Form 410, to change the committee's name. The
committee has an outstanding loan of $2,000 and an ending cash on
hand of $215.

QUESTIONS

(1) Rather than creating a new committee for Mr. Carroll's
supervisorial race, may the Form 410, Statement of Organization
for his mayoral committee be amended to change the name of the
controlled committee and the office sought and still retain the
original identification number assigned by the Secretary of
State's office?

1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Garland R. Porter
Page Two


and (2) May the current mayoral committee's ending cash on hand
and outstanding loan be transferred to the supervisorial
committee?

CONCLUSIONS

(1) Mr. Carroll may not use his mayoral committee for his
supervisorial campaign. He may not use the mayoral committee's
existing funds in connection with his campaign for supervisor.

Before soliciting or raising any funds for his supervisorial race,
Mr. Carroll must file a Form 501, Candidate Intention Statement.
A Form 502, Campaign Bank Account Statement must be filed within
24 hours of opening a bank account for his supervisorial race.
The Forms 501 and 502 are filed with the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If Mr. Carroll receives contributions of $1,000 or
more in connection with his supervisorial race, he must form a new
committee by filing another Form 410.

(2) The $215 cash on hand may not be used to support Mr.
Carroll's candidacy for supervisor. The funds must be left in the
mayoral account to be used for any lawful purpose other than
supporting his candidacy or supporting or opposing another
person's candidacy. For example, the $215 may be used to pay off
the mayoral committee's debts. The outstanding loan of $2,000 will
remain in this account until it is paid off. If Mr. Carroll
intends to raise funds to pay off the $2,000 loan, he must file a
Form 501 for this purpose prior to soliciting or receiving any
contributions.

ANALYSIS

Proposition 73, which was passed by the voters on June 7,
1988, amended the Political Reform Act, and provides:

Any person who possesses campaign funds on the
effective date of this chapter may expend these
funds for any lawful purpose other than to
support or oppose a candidacy for elective office.

(Section 85306.)

The Fair Political Practices Commission adopted regulations
providing for the use of certain funds received prior to January
1, 1989. However, the Los Angeles County Superior Court recently
held that the Commission's regulations were invalid. Pending
further action by the Commission or by an appellate court, the
effect of this ruling is that campaign funds received prior to
January 1, 1989, may not be used to support or oppose a candidacy.

Garland R. Porter
Page Three


Campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may be used
for any other lawful purpose.  Regulation 18536.2 copy enclosed,
provides guidance for determining the meaning of "lawful purpose."
Section 18536.2(a)  states:

     As used in Government Code Section 85306, "lawful
     purpose" means any purpose other than personal
     use, as defined in Chapter 5 (commencing with
     Section 12400) of Division 9 of the Elections
     Code.

Elections Code Section 12400 et seq. prohibits personal use of
campaign funds and sets out the permissible uses of surplus
campaign funds.  For an interpretation of the Elections Code you
must contact your local district attorney or the state Attorney
General's office.  The Fair Political Practices Commission does
not interpret the Elections Code.

Prior to solicitation or receipt of any funds, Mr. Carroll
must file a Statement of Intention, Form 501.  Section 85200 was
also added to the Political Reform Act by Proposition 73, and
provides:

     Prior to the solicitation or receipt of any
     contribution or loan, an individual who intends
     to be a candidate for an elective office shall
     file with the commission a statement signed under
     penalty of perjury of intention to be a candidate for
     a specific office.

In addition, Mr. Carroll must establish a bank account in a
financial institution located in California.  A Form 502, Campaign
Bank Account Statement, must be filed within 24 hours of
establishing the campaign bank account. Section 85201 provides:

     (a)  Upon the filing of the statement of intention
     pursuant to Section 85200, the individual shall
     establish one campaign contribution account at an
     office of a financial institution located in the
     state.

     (b)  Upon the establishment of an account, the name of
     the financial institution, the specific location, and
     the account number shall be filed with the commission
     within 24 hours.

     (d)  Any personal funds which will be utilized to
     promote the election of the candidate shall be
     deposited in the account prior to expenditure.

Garland R. Porter
Page Four


                    (e)  All campaign expenditures shall be made from
            the account.

        The $2,000 outstanding loan must be carried forward on Line 2
of the mayoral committee's summary page year to year until the
debt is repaid.  (1989 "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions of the Political Reform Act," page 78.)



        Enclosed are the following materials:

    The 1989 "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure
Provisions of the Political Reform Act."

    Form 501, Candidate Intention and Form 502, Campaign Bank
Account.

    Form 410, Statement of Organization.

    The Interim Information Manual on Proposition 68 and
Proposition 73 will be sent to you as soon as it is reproduced.


    If you have any questions concerning the advice provided in
this letter, please contact me at 916/322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Diane M. Griffiths
                                        General Counsel

                                        By: Mary Ann Kvasager
                                            Political Reform Consultant

Enclosures

Re: Letter No. 89-078

Dear Mr. Porter:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on February 1, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly. Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice. If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the
information needed. If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can. (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

Jeanne Pritchard
Chief Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division

JP:ld


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

267 RALEIGH DR.
VACAVILLE, CA 95687

TO: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J. STREET, SUITE 800
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814

RE: COMMITTEE TO RE-ELECT MAYOR
BILL CARROLL-ID NUMBER 600073

ON FEBRUARY 3, 1989 VACAVILLE MAYOR
WILLIAM J. CARROLL WILL ANNOUNCE
HIS CANDIDACY FOR SUPERVISOR OF THE
4TH DISTRICT, SOLANO COUNTY ELECTION
TO BE HELD IN JUNE 1990.

PLEASE FURNISH ADVICE ON THE
FOLLOWING:

CAN THE COMMITTEE'S FORM 410 BE AMENDED
TO CHANGE THE NAME OF THE COMMITTEE AND
CONTINUE TO USED THE SAME ID NUMBER ?

CAN CASH ON HAND $215 BE TRANSFERRED TO

sections.question:
(1) Rather than creating a new committee for Mr. Carroll's supervisorial race, may the Form 410, Statement of Organization for his mayoral committee be amended to change the name of the controlled committee and the office sought and still retain the original identification number assigned by the Secretary of State's office?

1, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

(2) May the current mayoral committee's ending cash on hand and outstanding loan be transferred to the supervisorial committee?
sections.conclusion:
(1) Mr. Carroll may not use his mayoral committee for his supervisorial campaign. He may not use the mayoral committee's existing funds in connection with his campaign for supervisor.

Before soliciting or raising any funds for his supervisorial race, Mr. Carroll must file a Form 501, Candidate Intention Statement. A Form 502, Campaign Bank Account Statement must be filed within 24 hours of opening a bank account for his supervisorial race. The Forms 501 and 502 are filed with the Fair Political Practices Commission. If Mr. Carroll receives contributions of $1,000 or more in connection with his supervisorial race, he must form a new committee by filing another Form 410.

(2) The $215 cash on hand may not be used to support Mr. Carroll's candidacy for supervisor. The funds must be left in the mayoral account to be used for any lawful purpose other than supporting his candidacy or supporting or opposing another person's candidacy. For example, the $215 may be used to pay off the mayoral committee's debts. The outstanding loan of $2,000 will remain in this account until it is paid off. If Mr. Carroll intends to raise funds to pay off the $2,000 loan, he must file a Form 501 for this purpose prior to soliciting or receiving any contributions.
sections.facts: You are the treasurer for the Committee to Re-Elect Mayor Bill Carroll. Mr. Carroll is currently the mayor of the City of Vacaville. Mr. Carroll will be seeking the office of supervisor for the 4th District, Solano County in a June, 1990, election. Mr. Carroll wants to retain his current mayoral committee's identification number and amend the committee's Statement of Organization, Form 410, to change the committee's name. The committee has an outstanding loan of $2,000 and an ending cash on hand of $215.
sections.analysis:
Proposition 73, which was passed by the voters on June 7, 1988, amended the Political Reform Act, and provides:

Any person who possesses campaign funds on the effective date of this chapter may expend these funds for any lawful purpose other than to support or oppose a candidacy for elective office.

(Section 85306.)

The Fair Political Practices Commission adopted regulations providing for the use of certain funds received prior to January 1, 1989. However, the Los Angeles County Superior Court recently held that the Commission's regulations were invalid. Pending further action by the Commission or by an appellate court, the effect of this ruling is that campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989, may not be used to support or oppose a candidacy.

Garland R. Porter
Page Three

Campaign funds received prior to January 1, 1989 may be used for any other lawful purpose. Regulation 18536.2, copy enclosed, provides guidance for determining the meaning of "lawful purpose." Section 18536.2(a) states:

As used in Government Code Section 85306, "lawful purpose" means any purpose other than personal use, as defined in Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 12400) of Division 9 of the Elections Code.

Elections Code Section 12400 et seq. prohibits personal use of campaign funds and sets out the permissible uses of surplus campaign funds. For an interpretation of the Elections Code you must contact your local district attorney or the state Attorney General's Office. The Fair Political Practices Commission does not interpret the Elections Code.

Prior to solicitation or receipt of any funds, Mr. Carroll must file a Statement of Intention, Form 501. Section 85200 was also added to the Political Reform Act by Proposition 73, and provides:

Prior to the solicitation or receipt of any contribution or loan, an individual who intends to be a candidate for an elective office shall file with the commission a statement signed under penalty of perjury of intention to be a candidate for a specific office.

In addition, Mr. Carroll must establish a bank account in a financial institution located in California. A Form 502, Campaign Bank Account Statement, must be filed within 24 hours of establishing the campaign bank account. Section 85201 provides:

(a) Upon the filing of the statement of intention pursuant to Section 85200, the individual shall establish one campaign contribution account at an office of a financial institution located in the state.

(b) Upon the establishment of an account, the name of the financial institution, the specific location, and the account number shall be filed with the commission within 24 hours.

(d) Any personal funds which will be utilized to promote the election of the candidate shall be deposited in the account prior to expenditure.

(e) All campaign expenditures shall be made from the account.

The $2,000 outstanding loan must be carried forward on Line 2 of the mayoral committee's summary page year to year until the debt is repaid. (1989 "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act," page 78.)

Enclosed are the following materials:

The 1989 "Information Manual on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act."

Form 501, Candidate Intention and Form 502, Campaign Bank Account.

Form 410, Statement of Organization.

The Interim Information Manual on Proposition 68 and Proposition 73 will be sent to you as soon as it is reproduced.

CONCLUSION: (1) Mr. Carroll may not use his mayoral committee for his supervisorial campaign. He may not use the mayoral committee's existing funds in connection with his campaign for supervisor.

Before soliciting or raising any funds for his supervisorial race, Mr. Carroll must file a Form 501, Candidate Intention Statement. (2) The $215 cash on hand may not be used to support Mr. Carroll's candidacy for supervisor. The funds must be left in the mayoral account to be used for any lawful purpose other than supporting his candidacy or supporting or opposing another person's candidacy. For example, the $215 may be used to pay off the mayoral committee's debts. The outstanding loan of $2,000 will remain in this account until it is paid off. If Mr. Carroll intends to raise funds to pay off the $2,000 loan, he must file a Form 501 for this purpose prior to soliciting or receiving any contributions.
embedding.first_500_words: **California Fair Political Practices Commission** March 22, 1989 Mr. Garland R. Porter Treasurer, Committee to Re-Elect Mayor Bill Carroll 267 Raleigh Drive Vacaville, CA 95687 RE: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-89-078 Dear Mr. Porter: You have requested advice under the campaign disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ FACTS You are the treasurer for the Committee to Re-Elect Mayor Bill Carroll. Mr. Carroll is currently the mayor of the City of Vacaville. Mr. Carroll will be seeking the office of supervisor for the 4th District, Solano County in a June, 1990, election. Mr. Carroll wants to retain his current mayoral committee's identification number and amend the committee's Statement of Organization, Form 410, to change the committee's name. The committee has an outstanding loan of $2,000 and an ending cash on hand of $215. QUESTIONS (1) Rather than creating a new committee for Mr. Carroll's supervisorial race, may the Form 410, Statement of Organization for his mayoral committee be amended to change the name of the controlled committee and the office sought and still retain the original identification number assigned by the Secretary of State's office? 1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations. (2) May the current mayoral committee's ending cash on hand and outstanding loan be transferred to the supervisorial committee? CONCLUSIONS (1) Mr. Carroll may not use his mayoral committee for his supervisorial campaign. He may not use the mayoral committee's existing funds in connection with his campaign for supervisor. Before soliciting or raising any funds for his supervisorial race, Mr. Carroll must file a Form 501, Candidate Intention Statement. A Form 502, Campaign Bank Account Statement must be filed within 24 hours of opening a bank account for his supervisorial race. The Forms 501 and 502 are filed with the Fair Political Practices Commission. If Mr. Carroll receives contributions of $1,000 or more in connection with his supervisorial race, he must form a new committee by filing another Form 410. (2) The $215 cash on hand may not be used to support Mr. Carroll's candidacy for supervisor. The funds must be left in the mayoral account to be used for any lawful purpose other than supporting his candidacy or supporting or opposing another person's candidacy. For example, the $215 may be used to pay off the mayoral committee's debts. The outstanding loan of $2,000 will remain in this account until it is paid off. If Mr. Carroll intends to raise funds to pay off the $2,000 loan, he must file a Form 501 for this purpose prior to soliciting or receiving any contributions.
